meta.title: A Day in the Life of a CCS Student
meta.description: Navigate the pressures of a Computer Science curriculum at AUF.

start.text: Dodo is rubbing his eyes while Cassie clicks rapidly in Figma. "D'yan na 'to pantay, Cass. If we use this grid, may 8-pixel gap sa dulo. It looks... messy. Pixel gore levels of messy."
start.speaker: Dodo

figma_crisis.text: Cassie sighs, "I know! We need the buttons to be perfectly square, zero leftover space in this 1920x1080 frame. Minimalist aesthetic nga 'di ba?"
figma_crisis.speaker: Cassie

gcd_logic.text: Dodo looks at the screen. "So we need the largest number that divides both 1920 and 1080? A Greatest Common Divisor? How do we find it fast? My brain is at 2% battery."
gcd_logic.speaker: Dodo

scene1_complete_euclid.text: Using remainders is blazing fast! The calculation works instantly, and the buttons fit perfectly into the 1920x1080 grid. "Pixel perfect!" Cassie exclaims. But wait, you hear a frantic knocking on the door. It's Inigo.
scene1_complete_euclid.speaker: Narrator

scene1_suboptimal.text: Dodo watches the screen compute. "Hmm, checking every integer downwards or listing prime factor trees works, but this is taking longer than expected. I wonder if there's a faster way to do this using remainders... Still, we got the buttons to fit perfectly!" Cassie cheers. But wait, you hear a frantic knocking on the door. It's Inigo.
scene1_suboptimal.speaker: Narrator

morning_panic.text: Inigo has one sock on and looks terrified. "I'm dead! I have to feed the cat, wash dishes, pack my bag, and fix the bed in 15 minutes! Pero 'di ko alam anong uunahin!"
morning_panic.speaker: Inigo

permutation_logic.text: Mikaela leans against the doorframe. "Chill, Inigo. You're frozen by decision paralysis. Do you even realize how many possible sequences you're trying to simulate?"
permutation_logic.speaker: Mikaela

end_chapter.text:
Inigo finally picks a path and starts moving. 24 permutations down to one successful morning. "Recursive thinking saved my life!" he shouts while running out.

*Mathematical Foundations Chapter Completed.*
end_chapter.speaker: Narrator

start.text: Cassie is staring intently at her laptop screen in VS Code. "Guys, our database login system keeps accepting duplicate user registrations. It's messing up the backend!"
start.speaker: Cassie

unique_suboptimal.text: Cassie runs the nested loop. "Hmm, comparing every registration against every other user is taking longer than expected. My CPU usage is spiking. I wonder if there's a faster way to do this with a Set or frequency tracker... But hey, no duplicates!" Just then, Mikaela bursts into the room. "The Alco quiz results are out! Whoever got the highest score has to treat the class!"
unique_suboptimal.speaker: Cassie

choices.label: Sort entire array to find max

unique_elements_node.text: "Good call! By checking if all usernames in the collection are distinct, we prevented duplicates," Cassie smiles. Just then, Mikaela bursts into the room holding up her phone. "The Alco quiz results are out! Whoever got the highest score has to treat the whole class to lunch!"
unique_elements_node.speaker: Mikaela

choices.label: Sort entire array to find max

max_suboptimal.text: Dodo watches the sorting algorithm execute. "Hmm, sorting the entire array just to find one top score is taking longer than expected. I wonder if there's a faster way by just iterating once O(N)... Wow, I got the highest score... goodbye allowance. Come on, let's head out. We need to catch a jeep at the terminal."
max_suboptimal.speaker: Dodo

max_element_node.text: "By iterating through the array and maintaining a variable tracking the largest value, we found the top scorer!" Dodo groans, "Wow, I got the highest score... goodbye allowance. Come on, let's head out. We need to catch a jeep at the terminal."
max_element_node.speaker: Dodo

sequential_search_node.text: At the bustling jeepney terminal, you scan linearly from start to finish to find the correct jeep route. You board successfully and arrive at the karaoke spot! Inigo is flipping through a massive, sorted karaoke songbook looking for his favorite OPM song. "There are thousands of songs here! How do we find it fast?"
sequential_search_node.speaker: Inigo

suboptimal_karaoke_node.text: Inigo flips through the massive songbook page by page. "Hmm, this linear scan is taking way longer than expected! I wonder if there's a faster way to do this by halving the book... Finally found it!" While Inigo sings his heart out, Cassie pulls out her phone to play an RPG. "I'm trying to add my friend's username in the guild search, but I only know part of their handle."
suboptimal_karaoke_node.speaker: Inigo

binary_search_node.text: By repeatedly dividing the sorted songbook interval in half O(log N), Inigo finds his OPM song efficiently! While Inigo sings his heart out, Cassie pulls out her phone to play a multiplayer RPG game. "I'm trying to add my friend's username in the guild search, but I only know part of their handle."
binary_search_node.speaker: Cassie

interpolation_search_node.text: Knowing the songbook is uniformly distributed alphabetically, you estimate the target position based on value. Instant match! While Inigo sings his heart out, Cassie pulls out her phone to play a multiplayer RPG game. "I'm trying to add my friend's username in the guild search, but I only know part of their handle."
interpolation_search_node.speaker: Cassie

bf_string_node.text: Aligning the pattern at every possible position until a match is found, Cassie successfully locates and adds her friend to the RPG guild party!
bf_string_node.speaker: Narrator

end_chapter.text:
From debugging login validation to finding jeepneys and belting out OPM hits, your searching and pattern matching skills saved the day!

*Searching & Pattern Matching Chapter Completed.*
end_chapter.speaker: Narrator

start.text: Dodo is staring at Figma, groaning. "Guys, my Figma asset panel is a complete disaster. I have all these cluttered UI icons, and I need to organize them by file size before I export. But doing it one by one feels like torture!"
start.speaker: Dodo

selection_sort_node.text: Dodo watches the icons swap. "Hmm, comparing and selecting them one by one O(N^2) is taking longer than expected, but my Figma assets are finally organized perfectly!" Cassie then calls everyone over. "Hey, I'm compiling a massive list of user data for our student management system. Before we can test the search functions, we need to sort thousands of entries by ID!"
selection_sort_node.speaker: Cassie

bubble_sort_node.text: Cassie tries stepping through the list, comparing adjacent elements and swapping them. "Ugh, O(N^2) is taking way too long for thousands of entries! My laptop fans are screaming!" Mikaela chimes in, "We need a stable divide-and-conquer approach to split the list into halves, sort them recursively, and merge them back together!"
bubble_sort_node.speaker: Mikaela

merge_sort_node.text: The merge sort compiles the list flawlessly and maintains stable ID ordering. Inigo looks at the code. "That was smooth! But what if we want an in-place efficient partition method? Picking a pivot to divide the array into smaller and larger elements?"
merge_sort_node.speaker: Inigo

quicksort_node.text: Quicksort partitions the array around the pivot and sorts the management system entries at blazing O(N log N) speed! Dodo rubs his chin, "What if memory space is strictly limited and we need a guaranteed priority queue approach? Like building a binary heap and extracting the max/min elements?"
quicksort_node.speaker: Dodo

heapsort_node.text: Building a binary heap allows you to extract elements in perfect priority order without using extra memory space! The student management system is now fully optimized and ready for deployment.
heapsort_node.speaker: Narrator

end_chapter.text:
From organizing Figma UI icons to sorting massive user databases with divide-and-conquer and heaps, your sorting algorithm mastery is unmatched!

*Sorting Algorithms Chapter Completed.*
end_chapter.speaker: Narrator

start.text: The panel members look stern. "Your deployment map has dependencies in a massive graph. How will you systematically traverse every module to prove there are no cycles?"
start.speaker: Panel Chair

graph_suboptimal.text: Randomly probing module dependencies causes redundant checks. "Hmm, checking without a structure is taking longer than expected and we almost got stuck in cycles. I wonder if there's a systematic BFS or DFS way to do this... Still, we scraped through!" The panelists give a cautious nod.
graph_suboptimal.speaker: Panel Chair

bfs_path.text:
Traversing layer-by-layer using a FIFO queue reveals all reachable system packages. The panelists look highly impressed.

"A clean, level-order traversal logic. Excellent defense!"
bfs_path.speaker: Panel Chair

dfs_path.text:
Plunging deep along each branch using a LIFO stack traces the recursive cycle pathways perfectly. The panelists nod in approval.

"Excellent back-tracking cycle-detection logic. Outstanding defense!"
dfs_path.speaker: Panel Chair

end_chapter.text:
Your capstone system defense is complete. Anya and Leo celebrate your O(V + E) success in the hallways.

*Chapter 4 Completed.*
end_chapter.speaker: Narrator

start.text: The clock is ticking in the 24-hour hackathon. Your navigation robot needs to find the optimal path in a grid representing a dense maze. Which heuristic fits?
start.speaker: Anya

astar_path.text:
Combining distance traveled g(n) and heuristic estimate h(n) leads the robot straight to the exit, bypassing all obstacles. The judges cheer!

"The optimal, shortest-path heuristic search in record time!"
astar_path.speaker: Judges

dfs_fail_path.text:
The robot wanders down the deepest blind alley, getting stuck in loops and taking way longer than expected. Time runs out.

"Hmm, blind DFS gets trapped in cycles without distance heuristics. I wonder if there's a faster, optimal way like A* Search..."
dfs_fail_path.speaker: Anya

end_chapter.text:
Your team took home 1st place in the hackathon. Heuristics lead to trophies.

*Chapter 5 Completed.*
end_chapter.speaker: Narrator

start.text: It is graduation day! AUF wants to issue secure digital certificates. How can we ensure they are both tamper-proof and signed?
start.speaker: System

crypto_path.text:
Hashing with SHA-256 guarantees integrity, and signing with private RSA keys guarantees authenticity. The registry clicks 'ISSUE'.

"Your credentials are secure for life! Congratulations, graduate!"
crypto_path.speaker: System

plaintext_path.text: Saving certificates in raw plaintext fails the security audit immediately. "Hmm, saving without cryptography leaves us vulnerable to tampering and takes longer to pass compliance audits. I wonder if there's a secure hashing and signing way to do this..."
plaintext_path.speaker: System

end_chapter.text:
You march across the stage, degree in hand, as your mechanical keyboard clicks in the distance. The AUF journey is complete.

*Chapter 6 Completed. You are the ultimate CCS student!*
end_chapter.speaker: Narrator

algorithms.title: DIJKSTRA'S SHORTEST PATH
algorithms.definition: An iterative algorithm that finds the shortest path between nodes in a weighted graph.
algorithms.useCase: Used primarily in network routing protocols and GPS navigation to calculate the fastest route.

algorithms.title: HASH MAP
algorithms.definition: An iterative algorithm that finds the optimal solution for complex computational problems.
algorithms.useCase: Used primarily in Hash Map related tasks and system optimizations.

algorithms.title: SELECTION SORT
algorithms.definition: Repeatedly finds the minimum element from the unsorted portion of a list and swaps it into its correct position.
algorithms.useCase: Drag and dropping unorganized and cluttered UI icons for your figma project by file size one by one.

algorithms.title: BUBBLE SORT
algorithms.definition: Repeatedly steps through the list, compares adjacent elements, and swaps them if they are in the wrong order.
algorithms.useCase: Compiling a massive list of user data for a management system. Sorting thousands of entries by ID.

algorithms.title: MERGE SORT
algorithms.definition: A divide-and-conquer algorithm that splits a list into halves, sorts them recursively, and merges the sorted halves back together.
algorithms.useCase: Compiling a massive list of user data for a management system. Sorting thousands of entries by ID stably.

algorithms.title: QUICKSORT
algorithms.definition: A divide-and-conquer algorithm that picks a "pivot" and partitions the array into elements smaller and larger than the pivot.
algorithms.useCase: Compiling a massive list of user data for a management system. Sorting thousands of entries by ID efficiently.

algorithms.title: HEAPSORT
algorithms.definition: A comparison-based sorting technique that builds a Binary Heap and repeatedly extracts the maximum/minimum element.
algorithms.useCase: Compiling a massive list of user data for a management system. Sorting thousands of entries by ID with priority heap.

algorithms.title: BINARY SEARCH
algorithms.definition: Efficiently finds a target in a sorted array by repeatedly dividing the search interval in half.
algorithms.useCase: Finding your favorite OPM song in a karaoke songbook.
algorithms.pseudocode:
function binarySearch(arr, target):
  low = 0, high = arr.length - 1
  while low <= high:
    mid = (low + high) / 2
    if arr[mid] == target: return mid
    else if arr[mid] < target: low = mid + 1
    else: high = mid - 1
  return -1

algorithms.title: SEQUENTIAL SEARCH
algorithms.definition: Scans a list linearly from start to finish to find a target value or determine its absence.
algorithms.useCase: Finding the correct jeep to take in a jeepney terminal.

algorithms.title: INTERPOLATION SEARCH
algorithms.definition: An improvement on binary search for uniformly distributed sorted data that estimates the target's position based on its value.
algorithms.useCase: Finding your favorite OPM song in a karaoke songbook.
algorithms.pseudocode:
function interpolationSearch(arr, target):
  low = 0, high = arr.length - 1
  while low <= high and target >= arr[low] and target <= arr[high]:
    pos = low + ((target - arr[low]) * (high - low)) / (arr[high] - arr[low])
    if arr[pos] == target: return pos
    else if arr[pos] < target: low = pos + 1
    else: high = pos - 1
  return -1

algorithms.title: MAX ELEMENT
algorithms.definition: Iterates through an array while maintaining a variable that tracks the largest value encountered.
algorithms.useCase: Finding who got the highest score in the alco quiz who will treat the whole class.

algorithms.title: UNIQUE ELEMENTS
algorithms.definition: Checks if all elements in a collection are distinct by comparing each pair or using a frequency tracker.
algorithms.useCase: Used in unique username login systems for the database system in VS code.

algorithms.title: BF STRING MATCHING
algorithms.definition: Checks for a pattern within a text by aligning it at every possible position until a match is found.
algorithms.useCase: Finding your friend to add him in the rpg game (Brute Force).
algorithms.pseudocode:
function bfStringMatch(text, pattern):
  n = text.length, m = pattern.length
  for i = 0 to n - m:
    j = 0
    while j < m and text[i + j] == pattern[j]: j++
    if j == m: return i
  return -1

algorithms.title: EUCLID'S ALGORITHM
algorithms.definition: An efficient method for computing the greatest common divisor (GCD) of two numbers by repeatedly replacing the larger number with the remainder of its division by the smaller number.
algorithms.useCase: Used in Cryptography (RSA), simplifying fractions, and optimizing UI grid layouts.

algorithms.title: CONSECUTIVE INTEGER CHECK
algorithms.definition: A brute-force method to find the GCD by checking all integers starting from the minimum of the two numbers downwards until a common divisor is found.
algorithms.useCase: Simple implementations where efficiency is not critical or for educational purposes.

algorithms.title: MIDDLE SCHOOL PROCEDURE
algorithms.definition: Finding the GCD by identifying the prime factorization of both numbers and multiplying the common prime factors together.
algorithms.useCase: Manual calculations and teaching the fundamental theorem of arithmetic.

algorithms.title: FACTORIAL (RECURSION)
algorithms.definition: Calculating the product of all positive integers up to n using a recursive function where f(n) = n * f(n-1).
algorithms.useCase: Calculating permutations, combinations, and understanding recursion.
